did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized an Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD), specifically the Deep Ensemble for the Recognition of Malignancy (DERM) algorithm (DERM v3), to analyze dermoscopic and macroscopic images of skin lesions. The AI was used to classify lesions as Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), melanoma, premalignant, or benign, and its diagnostic performance (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity) was evaluated against histopathology ground truth in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and describes a prospective, multi-center, single-arm, masked clinical study (DERM-003) involving 572 patients. It presents original experimental data, including patient demographics, lesion counts, and diagnostic performance metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity) of an AI-based Digital Health Technology compared to histopathology ground truth.

title: Effectiveness of an image analyzing AI-based Digital Health Technology to identify Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer and other skin lesions: results of the DERM-003 study
brief_summary: This prospective, multi-center study evaluated the effectiveness of an AI-based Digital Health Technology (AIaMD), specifically the DERM v3 algorithm, in identifying Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and other skin lesions. The study recruited 572 patients from four UK NHS Trusts, who provided dermoscopic images of suspicious lesions using three different smartphone cameras (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, and Samsung 10). The AI's diagnostic performance was assessed against histopathology ground truth for biopsied lesions and clinical diagnosis for non-biopsied lesions. The AIaMD achieved high AUROCs for SCC and BCC across all devices, ranging from 0.85 to 0.89, and correctly classified all 16 melanoma lesions. Although the study did not meet its co-primary endpoints for sensitivity and specificity thresholds, the AIaMD demonstrated diagnostic accuracy comparable to dermatologists, suggesting its potential to support timely diagnosis and triage in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for the AIaMD. 47 biopsied lesions diagnosed as SCC in the results; 43 in the Per Protocol (PP) population by histopathology; 22 additional lesions diagnosed as SCC by clinical diagnosis only. AUROC for SCC ranged from 0.85 to 0.89 across devices.

Primary target class for the AIaMD. 184 biopsied lesions diagnosed as BCC in the results; 176 in the PP population by histopathology; 22 additional lesions diagnosed as BCC by clinical diagnosis only. AUROC for BCC ranged from 0.87 to 0.89 across devices.
Target class for the AIaMD. 16 lesions diagnosed as melanoma in the study were correctly classified by the AIaMD. AUROC for melanoma was ≥91.8% for all cameras.

Secondary objective to identify premalignant conditions. The AIaMD is designed to detect these alongside cancers. Specific counts not provided in the main text, but included in the 'benign' or 'other' AUROC calculations.

Secondary objective to identify benign conditions. The AIaMD detects selected benign lesions such as intraepidermal carcinoma (IEC/SCC in situ), actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, and benign melanocytic nevi. AUROC for benign lesions ranged between 74.9–76.8% (biopsied only) and 79.8–80.9% (all lesions).

paper_terms: Spitzoid tumor of uncertain malignant potential
lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the study population. Two lesions diagnosed as other malignancies: one Neuroendocrine and one Spitzoid tumor of uncertain malignant potential (STUMP). Correctly classified by the AIaMD as malignant.
Broad category encompassing SCC and BCC, which are the most common NMSC types. The study title and introduction focus on identifying NMSC.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, premalignant, and benign lesions).
all_task_types.details: The AIaMD (DERM v3) classifies dermoscopic and macroscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories. The study evaluates performance using AUROC, sensitivity, and specificity for specific classes (SCC, BCC, melanoma) and benign/premalignant conditions. The model uses a hierarchy where it identifies lesions as potentially malignant or benign, with specific outputs for SCC, BCC, and melanoma.

datasets_used.name: DERM-003 Study Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective, multi-center, single-arm, masked clinical study dataset recruited from 4 UK NHS Trusts (University Hospitals Dorset, University Hospitals Sussex NHS Foundation Trust, Barnet and Chase Farm Hospitals, Royal Free London NHS Foundation Trust). It consists of dermoscopic and macroscopic images of suspicious skin lesions captured using three smartphone cameras (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, Samsung 10) with a DL1 dermoscopic lens attachment. Ground truth was provided by histopathology for biopsied lesions and clinical diagnosis for non-biopsied lesions.
classes_used: Premalignant lesions (e.g., Actinic Keratosis, Intraepidermal Carcinoma)
Benign lesions (e.g., Seborrheic Keratosis, Benign Melanocytic Nevi)
Other malignancies (e.g., Neuroendocrine, Spitzoid tumor of uncertain malignant potential)

use.testing: The dataset was used to evaluate the diagnostic performance (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity) of the DERM v3 AIaMD against histopathology ground truth (for biopsied lesions) and clinical diagnosis (for non-biopsied lesions).
use.other: The dataset was used to assess the AIaMD's performance across different smartphone hardware (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, Samsung 10) and to compare AIaMD performance against dermatologist clinical diagnoses.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operator Characteristic)

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operator Characteristic (AUROC) curves
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To examine the association of the algorithm’s confidence scale with histopathology-confirmed diagnosis (biopsied lesions) or clinical diagnosis (non-biopsied lesions) and to evaluate diagnostic performance for SCC, BCC, melanoma, and benign lesions.

To report statistical estimates of accuracy, including AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), false-positive rates (FPR), and false-negative rates (FNR).
To compare the AUROCs produced by the AIaMD against those produced by clinicians and to determine statistical significance of differences in diagnostic accuracy.
All statistical tests were conducted as two-tailed, with a significance threshold of p < 0.05.
To measure the level of agreement between clinical and histopathology diagnosis labels.
To assess concordance between AIaMD output labels using different camera combinations and between clinical and histopathology diagnoses.

statistical_methods_used.method: Sample size calculation based on power analysis
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the required number of SCC and BCC lesions to demonstrate AUROCs superior to 0.9 at alpha = 0.05 with 90% power, assuming a true AUROC of 0.98.
Software used to conduct statistical analysis.

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-tailed tests comparing AUROCs (specifically comparing AIaMD AUROCs against clinician AUROCs) and Cohen's kappa for agreement between clinical and histopathology diagnosis labels.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference between the AUROC produced by the AIaMD and the AUROC produced by clinicians; or there is no agreement between clinical and histopathology diagnosis labels beyond chance.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the AUROC produced by the AIaMD and the AUROC produced by clinicians (specifically that AIaMD AUROCs are higher); or there is significant agreement between clinical and histopathology diagnosis labels.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value of <0.05 was regarded as statistically significant. The study found that AUROCs for SCC lesions were significantly lower for clinicians than for the AIaMD (p < 0.026 for each camera) and that clinician AUROCs were significantly lower than AIaMD for IEC, AK, and benign lesions (p ≤ 0.04). A weak to moderate level of agreement was found between clinical and histopathology diagnosis labels (Cohen’s kappa = 0.52, p < 0.001).

DERM-003 Study Cohort (Biopsied Lesions.metric: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operator Characteristic)
DERM-003 Study Cohort (Biopsied Lesions.notes: The AIaMD AUROCs for SCC lesions were significantly lower for clinicians than for the AIaMD (p < 0.026 for each camera). Clinician AUROCs were also significantly lower than AIaMD (p ≤ 0.04) for lesions diagnosed as IEC, AK, and benign by histopathology. A weak to moderate level of agreement between clinical and histopathology diagnosis labels was found (Cohen’s kappa = 0.52, p < 0.001).

DERM-003 Study Cohort (All Lesions - Int.metric: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operator Characteristic)
DERM-003 Study Cohort (All Lesions - Int.notes: The AUROC for benign lesions when all lesions were assessed ranged between 79.8–80.9%. The AUROC for melanoma was ≥91.8% for all cameras when the AIaMD assessed both biopsied lesions and all lesions.

DERM-003 Study Cohort (Diagnostic Thresh.notes: Using pre-determined diagnostic thresholds on images taken on the iPhone 6S. All 16 lesions diagnosed as melanoma in the study were correctly classified by the AIaMD. Both 'other malignant' lesions (Neuroendocrine and STUMP) were classified as malignant by the AIaMD using images from all cameras.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Ensemble for the Recognition of Malignancy (DERM) algorithm, specifically version 3 (DERM v3)

architecture_details.model_type: Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD)
architecture_details.hierarchy: The algorithm applies a hierarchy where if a lesion is identified as potentially either Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) or melanoma, it returns the classification of melanoma. For a lesion diagnosed as Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), an output of 'suspected melanoma' is considered a true positive, whereas for a lesion diagnosed as melanoma, an output of 'suspected SCC' is a false negative.

classification_or_output_head.confidence_scale: The algorithm produces a confidence scale used to calculate AUROC and determine sensitivity/specificity using pre-determined diagnostic thresholds.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep Ensemble for the Recognition of Malignancy (DERM) algorithm
conclusions: The AI-based Digital Health Technology (AIaMD, DERM v3) demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for identifying Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) across different smartphone hardware, with AUROCs ranging from 0.85 to 0.89 for biopsied lesions.
The AIaMD achieved an AUROC of ≥91.8% for melanoma detection across all tested cameras and correctly classified all 16 melanoma lesions in the study.
The AIaMD's diagnostic performance for SCC and BCC was at least comparable to, and in the case of SCC significantly superior to, that of dermatologists (clinicians).
The study did not meet its co-primary endpoints for sensitivity and specificity thresholds, which were based on optimistic in silico performance assumptions (true AUROC of 98%).

The AIaMD showed good generalizability across different smartphone cameras (iPhone 6S, iPhone 11, Samsung 10) and operating systems, although there was only moderate concordance between outputs from different hardware.
The study population was limited to secondary care dermatology clinics, which may not be fully representative of all patients who would use the technology in primary care or other settings.
The AIaMD has the potential to support the timely diagnosis and triage of malignant and premalignant skin lesions in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study failed to meet its co-primary endpoints for sensitivity and specificity, which were based on optimistic in silico performance assumptions (true AUROC of 98%) that did not reflect real-world clinical data.

The study population was limited to UK secondary care dermatology clinics, consisting of 96.9% Fitzpatrick skin types I-III, with no skin cancers diagnosed in patients with Fitzpatrick skin types V and VI, limiting generalizability to darker skin tones.

For non-biopsied lesions, clinical diagnosis was used as the ground truth, which may artificially inflate the apparent performance of the AIaMD and introduces potential bias compared to using a panel consensus or histopathology.
The AIaMD demonstrated high sensitivity for malignant lesions (e.g., 98% for SCC, 94% for BCC) but low specificity (38% for SCC, 28% for BCC), indicating a high false-positive rate.
All 16 melanoma lesions and 2 'other malignant' lesions (STUMP and neuroendocrine) were correctly classified by the AIaMD, demonstrating robust detection of rare malignancies.

The study was conducted in a masked setting with a single dermatologist providing clinical diagnoses, which may not reflect the variability of clinical practice or the utility of the tool for non-specialists.
Moderate concordance was observed between AIaMD outputs from different smartphone cameras (60.3% agreement between iPhone 6S and Samsung 10), though AUROCs remained high across devices.
The AIaMD is a Class IIa UKCA marked medical device already deployed in UK clinical pathways since 2020, providing evidence of real-world deployment context.
